sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, a 57-year-old male, presents with a couple of weeks of shortness of breath, which occurs when he wakes up and persists throughout the day.
He reports coughing up a significant amount of blood, approximately 3-4 cups per day, for the past three weeks.
He has a history of diabetes, hypertension, and asthma.
He has been taking metformin 500mg twice a day for his diabetes and Norvasc for his hypertension.
He has also been experiencing knee pain for an extended period, but it is not related to his current symptoms.
Review of systems: The patient reports coughing up blood, but denies any fever, bruising, bleeding, or swelling.
Current medications: Metformin 500mg twice a day for diabetes, Norvasc (dosage not specified) for hypertension.
Objective:
Vital signs: Blood pressure 120/70, pulse 94% oxygen saturation.
Physical exam: No JVD, no carotid bruits, rails and rhonchi on the right lung, diminished breath sounds on the right side, 2/6 systolic ejection murmur, 1+ nonpitting edema on both lower extremities.
Test Results: [NOT MENTIONED]
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient presents with acute shortness of breath, coughing up significant amounts of blood, and a history of diabetes and hypertension.
Problem list: Pneumonia suspected; Uncontrolled diabetes; Hypertension.
Plan:

For the shortness of breath and coughing up blood, I will refer the patient to the emergency department for a chest x-ray, CT scan, and blood work to evaluate for a potential blood clot or other underlying cause.
I will hold off on prescribing Norvasc for the hypertension until the acute episode is under control.
I will also order a hemoglobin A1c to assess the patient's diabetes control.
For the diabetes, I will increase the patient's metformin dose to 750mg twice a day to improve glycemic control.
I will schedule a follow-up appointment in three weeks to discuss the patient's diabetes treatment plan.
Follow-up: The patient will follow up in three weeks to discuss diabetes treatment plan.
